title: A guide to the 'Foreign Earned Income Exclusion' for Americans working on a yacht

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Prove your 'tax home' is foreign by severing U.S. personal ties like driver's licenses and voter registrations, as maintaining a U.S. 'abode' will automatically disqualify your entire FEIE claim.
Maintain a meticulous daily travel log to prove 330 full days outside the U.S., strategically counting full days spent in international waters as qualifying 'foreign days'.
The Foreign Earned Income Exclusion is not automatic; you must file a U.S. tax return with Form 2555 to claim it, even if you believe no tax is due.
Recognize that the federal FEIE offers no protection from state income tax; you must take separate, formal actions to terminate your state residency to avoid a surprise tax liability.
summary: To address the significant tax anxiety and penalty risks U.S. yacht crew face, this guide provides a professional framework for claiming the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE). The core advice is to establish a foreign "tax home" by severing U.S. ties, meticulously track your calendar to meet the 330-day Physical Presence Test by counting days in international waters, and file annually with audit-proof documentation. By implementing this disciplined system, crew can confidently secure the tax exclusion, eliminate compliance risks, and achieve financial peace of mind.
ai_overview_answer: To qualify for the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), U.S. yacht crew must follow a three-step framework focused on documentation and meticulous planning. First, you must establish a "tax home" outside the U.S. by proving your vessel is your principal place of business and severing ties to any U.S. "abode" or permanent dwelling. Next, you must pass the Physical Presence Test by being physically outside the United States for at least 330 full days in any 12-month period. Critically, full days spent in international waters count as qualifying foreign days, while time in U.S. shipyards or territories does not. To claim the exclusion, you must file a U.S. tax return (Form 1040) with Form 2555 attached, even if no tax is due. Maintaining a detailed, day-by-day travel log is the most important evidence for audit-proofing your claim. Finally, remember the FEIE is a federal exclusion and does not eliminate separate state tax obligations, which require formally terminating state residency.

itemListElement.name: A guide to the 'Foreign Earned Income Exclusion' for Americans working on a yacht

@graph.name: How to Qualify for the FEIE as U.S. Yacht Crew
@graph.description: A 3-step framework for U.S. yacht crew to navigate the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), covering the Tax Home Test, Physical Presence Test, and proper filing procedures.

step.name: Establish Your Foundation (The Tax Home Test)
step.text: Prove your 'tax home' is outside the United States by establishing a foreign headquarters for your career. This involves leveraging your Seaman's Employment Agreement to designate a foreign home port, building a 'center of gravity' with foreign bank accounts and memberships, and actively severing U.S. ties like driver's licenses and voter registrations to avoid the 'abode' trap.

step.name: Master Your Calendar (The Physical Presence Test)
step.text: Pass the Physical Presence Test by being physically outside the U.S. for at least 330 full days in any 12-month period. Critically, count full days in international waters as qualifying foreign days. Maintain a 'bulletproof travel log' to document your location daily and be mindful of common traps like U.S. shipyard periods and time spent in U.S. territories, which do not count as foreign days.

step.name: File with Confidence (The Audit-Proofing Process)
step.text: Proactively claim the FEIE by filing a U.S. tax return (Form 1040) with Form 2555 attached, even if you owe no tax. Assemble an 'audit defense kit' with your travel log, employment contracts, and vessel itineraries. Strategically choose the 12-month period that maximizes your foreign days and take formal steps to terminate state residency, as the FEIE is a federal-only exclusion.

mainEntity.name: What's the difference between the Physical Presence Test and the Bona Fide Residence Test?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Physical Presence Test, which this guide focuses on, is a purely mathematical test of days outside the U.S. It's the most common and straightforward path for yacht crew. The Bona Fide Residence Test is more subjective; it requires you to prove you were a resident of a foreign country for an entire tax year. This often involves stronger ties like renting an apartment and paying local taxes, making it less suitable for transient crew but a potential option for those based long-term in a single foreign country.

mainEntity.name: Can I still have a U.S. mailing address or bank account?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can, but it must be managed carefully. Using a U.S. mail-forwarding service for convenience is generally acceptable. Maintaining a U.S. bank account for investments or to receive payments is also common. However, these must not be your *primary* financial or personal hubs. If your only bank account is in the U.S., your driver's license is tied to a U.S. address you use between trips, and all your mail goes there, the IRS could argue you maintain a U.S. 'abode,' jeopardizing your claim. The key is that your financial center of gravity must demonstrably shift abroad.

mainEntity.name: My salary is paid into my U.S. bank account. Is that still 'foreign earned income'?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. 'Foreign earned income' is determined by where you physically perform the services, not where the funds are deposited. As long as you earned your salary while working on a vessel outside the United States, it qualifies, regardless of whether your employer pays you in dollars, euros, or deposits the funds into a bank in Florida.

mainEntity.name: What happens if I fail the Physical Presence Test by a few days?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 330-day requirement is absolute. If you are present in a foreign country for only 329 days in your chosen 12-month period, you fail the test for that period and cannot claim the FEIE. This is why meticulous calendar management is not just a suggestion but a critical operational requirement. A few unplanned days in a Fort Lauderdale shipyard could cost you tens of thousands of dollars in federal tax.

body.content: The anxiety that plagues many U.S. yacht crew—that persistent, low-grade fear of a tax misstep resulting in catastrophic penalties—stems not from the complexity of the rules, but from the lack of a professional system. As the CEO of your global career, a "Business-of-One," you require an operations manual for compliance.

This framework provides that manual. By shifting from a defensive posture of uncertainty to an offensive position of control, you can navigate the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) with the same precision you apply on deck. It’s not about finding loopholes; it’s about professional execution.
Step 1: Establish Your Foundation (The Tax Home Test)

Your entire claim to the FEIE rests on a single, powerful concept: proving your "tax home" is outside the United States. Get this right, and you have a defensible position. Get it wrong, and your entire compliance strategy crumbles. This requires a deliberate shift in mindset, moving from the conventional idea of "home" to the strategic view of a CEO establishing a foreign headquarters.
Reframe "Home" as "Headquarters"

First, understand the IRS definition of a tax home: it is your principal place of business or employment, not where you maintain a personal residence. This distinction is your primary strategic advantage. Since your workplace is a globally mobile vessel, your core mission is to document that vessel—and its foreign home port—as the official headquarters of your career. If you have neither a main place of business nor a regular place you live, the IRS considers you an "itinerant," and your tax home is simply wherever you work. For a professional yachtie, this means the yacht itself is your operational base.
Leverage Your Seaman's Employment Agreement (SEA)

Your employment contract is the cornerstone of your evidence. An auditor will look for objective proof, and your SEA provides it. To make it a powerful tool in your defense, ensure it explicitly contains:

items: **The Vessel’s Foreign Flag:** Clearly states the yacht's country of registration.
**Primary Area of Operation:** Designates a foreign region (e.g., "The Mediterranean," "Caribbean Basin").
**Designated Foreign Home Port:** Names a specific foreign port (e.g., Palma de Mallorca, Spain; Antibes, France) as the vessel's base of operations.

body.content: This contract transforms your presence abroad from a temporary visit into a formal, work-related assignment, directly supporting your foreign tax home.
Build a "Center of Gravity" Abroad

Even without a traditional apartment lease, you must create a web of financial and personal connections that anchor your life to a foreign location. These are the small, consistent actions that demonstrate your life is genuinely centered overseas. Actionable steps include:

items: Opening a bank account in your vessel’s home port or a crew-friendly jurisdiction.
Obtaining a local mobile phone number and using a foreign mailing address for correspondence.
Joining a local yacht club, gym, or professional maritime organization.

body.content: Avoid the "Abode" Trap

While you build foreign ties, you must actively and demonstrably sever domestic ones. The IRS will deny your foreign tax home if you maintain an "abode" in the United States. An abode is defined by your personal and family ties—your home, habitation, or place of dwelling. Maintaining a U.S. abode makes you ineligible for the FEIE.

This means you cannot have a house or apartment continuously available for your use, hold a U.S. driver's license with a family address, or remain registered to vote at a previous U.S. residence. This isn't just about being physically absent; it's about methodically dismantling your stateside life to prove your new headquarters—and your life—is truly abroad.
Step 2: Master Your Calendar (The Physical Presence Test)

With your foreign tax home established as a solid foundation, you must shift from strategy to execution. This is where your claim is won or lost: in the disciplined, day-to-day management of your calendar. For professional yacht crew, the Physical Presence Test is the most direct path to qualifying for the FEIE. Mastering it requires you to operate with the precision of a navigator, because every single day matters.

To pass, you must be physically present in a foreign country or countries for at least 330 full days during *any* consecutive 12-month period. A "full day" is a strict 24-hour period from midnight to midnight.
The Definitive Rule on "International Waters"

This is the single most powerful tool in your tax toolkit. The IRS defines a "foreign country" for this test as any territory under the sovereignty of a government other than the United States. Your goal is not to prove you were *in* a specific country, but to prove you were *outside* the U.S.

Therefore, a full day spent in international waters is a qualifying foreign day. This transforms transatlantic crossings and long passages from logistical necessities into strategic assets for your FEIE claim.

rows: The entire 24-hour period was spent outside the U.S.
Part of the 24-hour period was spent inside the U.S.
Part of the 24-hour period was spent inside the U.S.
The entire 24-hour period was spent outside the U.S.

body.content: Create a "Bulletproof Travel Log"

Your passport stamps are historical markers, not a sufficient defense. An IRS auditor requires a contemporaneous, detailed record of your location for every day of the tax year. This log is your single most important piece of evidence. Use a simple spreadsheet or a dedicated app to track the following daily:

items: **Date**
**Location** (Port/City/Country or "International Waters")
**Vessel Name**
**Notes** (Specifically log any full days spent in the U.S. or its territories)

body.content: Navigate Common Traps
The rules have sharp edges. A few miscalculated days can invalidate your entire claim.

items: **The Shipyard Trap:** Any full day spent in a U.S. shipyard (e.g., Fort Lauderdale, San Diego) is a U.S. day and breaks your 330-day count. Plan your personal travel meticulously around these yard periods.
**The U.S. Territory Blind Spot:** Days spent in Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, or American Samoa are **not** foreign days. For tax purposes, you have re-entered the United States.

**The "Fly-Through" Rule:** If you are in transit between two foreign points and are physically in the U.S. for less than 24 hours (e.g., a layover in Miami), that day is *not* treated as a day in the U.S. Keep your flight itineraries as concrete proof.

body.content: Step 3: File with Confidence (The Audit-Proofing Process)

Meticulously tracking your days is how you qualify, but the process isn't complete until you file correctly and build a defense against potential scrutiny. This final step secures the financial benefit of your disciplined record-keeping.

items: **The "No Tax Due" Filing Imperative:** The FEIE is not automatic; you must proactively claim it. To do so, you **must** file a standard U.S. tax return (Form 1040) and attach Form 2555, even if you believe you owe no tax. Failing to file can lead to substantial penalties and, in some cases, the IRS can revoke your right to claim the exclusion for that year.
**Assemble Your "Audit Defense Kit":** Your goal is to make any potential IRS inquiry an open-and-shut case. Have a dedicated digital folder organized and ready to deploy, including:
Your complete, day-by-day travel log.
Scanned copies of all SEAs for the period.
Flight itineraries and boarding passes for any travel to or from the U.S.
Official vessel itineraries or a signed letter from the captain confirming your dates of service and cruising locations.

**Choose Your 12-Month Period Strategically:** The test applies to *any* consecutive 12-month period, not just the calendar year. Analyze your travel log to identify the rolling 12-month window that contains the maximum number of qualifying foreign days, thereby maximizing the income you can exclude.

**Understand State-Level Obligations:** The FEIE is a *federal* exclusion and has no bearing on state tax. States like California, New York, Virginia, and New Mexico are notoriously aggressive in pursuing income tax from former residents. You must take formal, documented steps to terminate your state residency—such as changing your voter registration, driver's license, and closing local bank accounts—to avoid a surprise state tax bill.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
What's the difference between the Physical Presence Test and the Bona Fide Residence Test?

The Physical Presence Test, which this guide focuses on, is a purely mathematical test of days outside the U.S. It's the most common and straightforward path for yacht crew. The Bona Fide Residence Test is more subjective; it requires you to prove you were a resident of a foreign country for an entire tax year. This often involves stronger ties like renting an apartment and paying local taxes, making it less suitable for transient crew but a potential option for those based long-term in a single foreign country.
Can I still have a U.S. mailing address or bank account?

You can, but it must be managed carefully. Using a U.S. mail-forwarding service for convenience is generally acceptable. Maintaining a U.S. bank account for investments or to receive payments is also common. However, these must not be your *primary* financial or personal hubs. If your only bank account is in the U.S., your driver's license is tied to a U.S. address you use between trips, and all your mail goes there, the IRS could argue you maintain a U.S. "abode," jeopardizing your claim. The key is that your financial center of gravity must demonstrably shift abroad.
My salary is paid into my U.S. bank account. Is that still "foreign earned income"?

Yes. "Foreign earned income" is determined by where you physically perform the services, not where the funds are deposited. As long as you earned your salary while working on a vessel outside the United States, it qualifies, regardless of whether your employer pays you in dollars, euros, or deposits the funds into a bank in Florida.
What happens if I fail the Physical Presence Test by a few days?

The 330-day requirement is absolute. If you are present in a foreign country for only 329 days in your chosen 12-month period, you fail the test for that period and cannot claim the FEIE. This is why meticulous calendar management is not just a suggestion but a critical operational requirement. A few unplanned days in a Fort Lauderdale shipyard could cost you tens of thousands of dollars in federal tax.
Master Your Compliance, Reclaim Your Peace of Mind

You’ve built a career on precision, foresight, and an unwavering commitment to professional standards. The skills required to navigate intricate maritime laws and manage complex onboard systems are the same skills required to manage your own financial headquarters.

This 3-step framework—establishing your foundational tax home, executing with a meticulous calendar, and fortifying your position with an audit-proof file—is your system for control. By applying the rigorous standards of your profession to your Business-of-One, you don’t just achieve compliance; you reclaim your focus for what truly matters—your career, your future, and the incredible journey of a life at sea.
